fg-alert.heading: You still need to complete earlier steps
fg-alert.p: To use the estimator, you’ll have to go back and complete Step 1: About You

div.p: Based on what you entered, we estimate that you’re withholding more taxes than you need to.
You’ll likely get a tax refund when you file your federal tax return.

p.span: If you’d like to get more take-home pay and reduce your estimated refund, you can adjust your withholding to $0.

div.p: If you keep your withholding the same, you’ll You’ll likely get a tax refund when you file your federal tax return.

p.span: If you’d like to get more take-home pay and reduce your estimated refund, you can adjust your withholding.

div.p: Based on what you entered, we estimate that you’re not withholding enough taxes.

If you keep your withholding the same, you’ll You'll likely owe taxes when you file your federal tax return. You may have to pay a penalty in addition to what you owe if you owe at least $1,000 and more than 10 percent of your tax bill.

p.span: If you’d like to get your tax balance closer to $0,
If you’d like to get your tax balance closer to $0 and avoid paying a penalty,

span.span: you can reduce your take-home pay by adjusting your withholding.
you can make estimated tax payments to make up for the difference between your withholding adjustment and tax owed.
you can reduce your take-home pay by adjusting your withholding and making estimated tax payments to make up for the difference between your withholding adjustment and tax owed.
you can make estimated tax payments to make up for the difference between your withholding adjustment and tax owed.

div.p: Based on what you entered, we estimate that you’re withholding enough to cover your federal taxes.
If you keep your withholding the same, it’s It’s likely you won’t owe federal taxes when you file your federal tax return.
If you adjust your withholding, your federal tax balance and take-home pay will change.
First, we calculated your taxable income.
Then, we applied the tax brackets to your taxable income to estimate your tax liability for your tax return.
Finally, we applied your credits and subtracted any payments you already made towards your federal taxes.
If you owe at least $1,000 and more than 10 percent of your tax bill, you may have to pay a penalty in addition to your federal tax owed.
Note: This estimate depends on the accuracy of your information. The IRS does not guarantee the accuracy of this estimate and accepts no liability resulting from your use of this estimation.

section.h3: How to adjust your withholding to get more take-home pay, which will reduce your estimated refund
How to adjust your withholding to get your tax balance closer to $0, which will reduce your take-home pay
section.p: Use our recommendations to complete Form W-4.
Use our recommendations to complete Form W-4P.
Use our recommendations to complete Forms W-4 and W-4P.

p.modal-link: What’s a Form W-4 and W-4P?

div.p: This will help you decide if you need to make any adjustments for the year.

modal-content.p: You need to submit a Form W-4 to each of your employers to help them withhold the right amount of taxes from your paychecks throughout the year.
Withholding at the right rate ensures that you don’t withhold too much and get smaller paychecks, or withhold too little and end up owing a large amount, or even penalties, at the end of the year.
You need to submit a Form W-4P to your pension or annuity providers to help them withhold the right amount of taxes from your payments throughout the year.
Withholding at the right rate ensures that you don’t withhold too much and get smaller payments, or withhold too little and end up owing a large amount, or even penalties, at the end of the year.

modal-dialog.modal-heading: What’s a Form W-4 and W-4P?

modal-content.p: You need to submit a Form W-4 to each of your employers or a Form W-4P to your pension or annuity providers to help them withhold the right amount of taxes from your paycheck or pension payment throughout the year.

Withholding at the right rate ensures that you don’t withhold too much and take home a smaller amount, or withhold too little and end up owing a large amount, or even penalties, at the end of the year.
This is the filing status you selected in Step 1 of the estimator.
Your filing status affects your standard deduction and which credits and deductions you’re eligible for.
This steps helps you decide which job or pension you should withhold from.
You’ve already completed this step by using the estimator.
This is where you can say if you have more than one job, or if you and your spouse (if filing jointly) both work.
Leave this unchecked because the estimator’s recommendation already takes multiple jobs (if present) into account so you don’t have to reveal this information to your employer.
Step 3 shows the total amount of credits you’re planning to claim in .
This total amount is based on the information you added to the estimator for your income, whether you have dependents, and which credits you think you’ll qualify for.
Step 4 accounts for any differences between the income you earn from this job and your other taxable income. The amounts entered here help to adjust your withholding to account for this difference.
Step 4(a) is used if you entered other sources of taxable income into the estimator that’s not from jobs, pensions, or annuities.

Step 4 accounts for any differences between the income you earn from this pension or annuity and your other taxable income. The amounts entered here help to adjust your withholding to account for this difference.
Step 4(a) is used if you entered other sources of taxable income into the estimator that’s not from jobs, pensions, or annuities.
Step 4 accounts for any differences between the income you earn from this job and your other taxable income. The amounts entered here help to adjust your withholding to account for this difference.
Step 4(b) includes the deductions and adjustments you entered into the estimator. These can help reduce how much of your income is taxed and the amount you need to withhold.

Step 4 accounts for any differences between the income you earn from this pension or annuity and your other taxable income. The amounts entered here help to adjust your withholding to account for this difference.
Step 4(b) includes the deductions and adjustments you entered into the estimator. These can help reduce how much of your income is taxed and the amount you need to withhold.
Step 4 accounts for any differences between the income you earn from this job and your other taxable income. The amounts entered here help to adjust your withholding to account for this difference.
Step 4(c) is used if you need to withhold more from your paycheck. Otherwise, this box may be left blank.

Step 4 accounts for any differences between the income you earn from this pension or annuity and your other taxable income. The amounts entered here help to adjust your withholding to account for this difference.
Step 4(c) is used if you need to withhold more from your payment. Otherwise, this box may be left blank.
